content.text: Before a coding agent can generate, edit, or refactor code, it must first build a mental model of the codebase it is working inside. Code understanding is the set of techniques an agent uses to extract structured, queryable knowledge from raw source files — covering everything from token-level text search to fully resolved type hierarchies — so that each action the agent takes is grounded in accurate facts about the existing code rather than hallucinated guesses.

attrs.alt: Code understanding pipeline: source code flows through lexical, syntactic, semantic, and contextual layers into a knowledge store queried by the coding agent via grep, LSP, AST, and vector search tools

content.text: Code understanding refers to the agent's ability to parse and interpret a software repository well enough to answer questions such as: "Where is this function defined?", "What types does this API accept?", "Which files import this module?", and "What does this class do?" These questions are prerequisites for every higher-level coding task — generation, debugging, refactoring, and test writing.

Unlike a human engineer who accumulates implicit knowledge over months of working in a repository, an agent must construct its understanding on demand, typically within a single context window. This requires a layered strategy: fast lexical search for identifiers, structured AST queries for syntax, Language Server Protocol (LSP) for semantic resolution, and embedding-based retrieval for conceptual similarity.

The depth of understanding needed scales with the task. Renaming a variable might only require text search, while extracting a class into a separate module requires knowing all callers, all type constraints, and all side effects. A well-designed coding agent selects the right level of analysis automatically based on the operation it is about to perform.

Coding agents that lack robust code understanding produce changes that are locally plausible but globally incorrect. Classic failure modes include importing a symbol that does not exist in the version currently in use, duplicating a utility function that already exists elsewhere in the codebase, using an API with incorrect argument types because the agent inferred the signature from a docstring rather than the actual type annotation, and breaking callers because it did not identify all the sites that depend on a changed function signature.

Strong code understanding eliminates an entire class of errors that would otherwise require human review to catch. It also enables agents to work confidently in large, unfamiliar codebases — not just toy scripts. The ability to navigate a 500,000-line monorepo, identify the relevant subsystem, understand the naming conventions and patterns in use, and make targeted surgical edits is what separates production-grade coding agents from simple code-completion tools.

From a practical standpoint, code understanding also reduces the number of round-trips the agent makes. When the agent can resolve a type, find a definition, or discover all callers in one tool call, it does not need to ask the user for clarification or emit speculative code that it revises multiple times. This cuts latency, token cost, and the risk of error propagation.
By resolving types, symbols, and call graphs before emitting edits, the agent avoids introducing type errors, import errors, or broken call sites that would fail compilation or tests.

Grounding LLM generations in real, retrieved facts (actual function signatures, existing module structure) is more reliable than relying on the model's training knowledge, which may not reflect the current state of the repository.

Targeted symbol lookups and semantic search retrieve only the relevant snippets, avoiding the need to load entire files into the context window. A well-designed understanding layer reduces token usage by 60–80% compared to naive full-file inclusion.
The agent can traverse the codebase independently — following imports, finding interface implementations, locating test files — without user guidance at each step.
By reading existing code patterns — naming conventions, error handling idioms, import style — the agent can generate additions that are stylistically consistent with the rest of the codebase.

Every tool call — grep, LSP hover, tree-sitter parse — adds round-trip time. Deep understanding of a complex subsystem may require dozens of sequential lookups, making the agent noticeably slower than a simple complete-and-submit approach.
Building and maintaining vector embeddings, symbol indexes, and AST caches for a large repository is expensive both in initial build time and in incremental update cost when files change.

LSP support and tree-sitter grammars exist for most mainstream languages but are incomplete or unavailable for legacy, domain-specific, or niche languages. The agent degrades to text-only search in those cases.

Indexes built before a recent set of edits may return stale results. If the agent edits a file and then immediately queries the index, it may see the pre-edit state unless the index is invalidated and rebuilt synchronously.

Even with smart retrieval, deeply interconnected code — where understanding one component requires understanding five others — can still exhaust the context window before the agent has enough information to act safely.
Code understanding proceeds in four progressively richer layers, each building on the one below.

The simplest and fastest level. The agent treats source files as text and uses regular expressions or substring search to find identifiers, string literals, or comment patterns. Tools like ripgrep can scan millions of lines per second. This layer suffices for tasks like "find all files that reference this configuration key" but cannot distinguish between a function definition and a string that happens to contain the function name.

The agent parses source code into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST). Each node in the tree corresponds to a language construct: function definition, class body, import statement, conditional expression. AST queries let the agent answer structural questions: "List every class that inherits from

", or "Find all function calls where the first argument is a string literal." The tree-sitter library provides incremental, error-tolerant parsing for 50+ languages and is the foundation of AST-based tools in modern editors and agents.

Semantics resolves names to definitions, infers or checks types, and tracks scopes. This is what the Language Server Protocol (LSP) provides. When an agent calls "go to definition" on a symbol, the language server returns the file path and line number of the declaration, regardless of how many layers of indirection or re-exports lie between the usage site and the definition. Type hover gives the full resolved type annotation. Find-references returns every usage. This layer is the primary source of ground-truth facts about the code's structure.

The deepest layer captures intent: what was the programmer trying to do, and why? Sources include docstrings, inline comments, commit messages, PR descriptions, linked issue bodies, and test names. This layer is primarily used by the LLM component of the agent rather than deterministic tools. The agent retrieves relevant context (via embedding search or keyword search on comments/docs) and includes it in the prompt alongside the structural facts from the layers above, letting the LLM reason about the purpose of the code it is about to modify.

The diagram above shows the complete pipeline. Source code from the repository feeds into the four understanding layers, which populate a knowledge store. The agent queries this store through a set of tool interfaces — ripgrep, LSP, tree-sitter, and vector search — rather than reading raw files directly. This separation is critical: it means the agent's queries are answered by structured indexes rather than linear file scans, enabling sub-second lookups over repositories with millions of lines.

The knowledge store has four primary components. The vector embedding index stores chunk-level embeddings (typically 512-token sliding windows with overlap) and supports approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) search for semantic queries. The symbol index maps each identifier to its definition location, type signature, and all reference sites. The dependency graph tracks which modules import which, enabling reachability analysis. The metadata store holds resolved type information, function signatures, docstrings, and git blame annotations.

Every coding agent task requires some degree of code understanding, but the required depth varies significantly. Simple one-file operations (writing a new unit test for an isolated function, adding a docstring) may need only lexical search to locate the function. Multi-file refactoring — renaming a public API, extracting a module, changing a data model — requires semantic-level understanding with call graph traversal.

Use deeper understanding layers when: the change crosses module boundaries, the task involves type-checked code, the codebase uses heavy abstraction (interfaces, decorators, metaclasses), or the task description is underspecified and the agent must infer intent from surrounding code. Stick to lexical search when speed is critical, the codebase is in a poorly-supported language, or the task is purely additive with no risk of breaking existing code.

Code understanding is less valuable — and potentially misleading — when the repository is very small (the entire codebase fits in context), when the language is dynamically typed to the point where static analysis provides little useful information, or when the codebase is so poorly structured that the index reflects inconsistent or incorrect patterns.
A practical code understanding implementation for a Python-based coding agent uses three tools in combination: ripgrep for fast text search, the Python tree-sitter bindings for AST queries, and the
language server (via subprocess) for semantic resolution. The agent exposes these as callable tools that the LLM can invoke during its reasoning loop.

pip install tree-sitter
# Build language grammars (one-time setup)
python - <<'EOF'
from tree_sitter import Language, Parser
import subprocess, os

# Clone the grammar repos
for lang in ['python', 'typescript', 'rust', 'go']:
    subprocess.run(
        ['git', 'clone', '--depth=1',
         f'https://github.com/tree-sitter/tree-sitter-{lang}'],
        check=True
    )

# Build a shared library containing all grammars
Language.build_library(
    'build/languages.so',
    [
        'tree-sitter-python',
        'tree-sitter-typescript/typescript',
        'tree-sitter-rust',
        'tree-sitter-go',
    ]
)
EOF

from tree_sitter import Language, Parser
from pathlib import Path
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

PY_LANGUAGE = Language('build/languages.so', 'python')

@dataclass
class SymbolLocation:
    file: str
    line: int          # 1-indexed
    col: int           # 0-indexed
    kind: str          # 'function' | 'class' | 'import'
    parent: Optional[str] = None

def find_definitions(root: Path, symbol: str) -> list[SymbolLocation]:
    """
    Return every top-level function/class definition whose name matches
     across all .py files under .
    """
    parser = Parser()
    parser.set_language(PY_LANGUAGE)

    # tree-sitter query: match (function_definition name: (identifier) @fn)
    query = PY_LANGUAGE.query("""
        (function_definition name: (identifier) @fn.name)
        (class_definition name: (identifier) @cls.name)
    """)

    results: list[SymbolLocation] = []
    for path in root.rglob('*.py'):
        source = path.read_bytes()
        tree = parser.parse(source)
        captures = query.captures(tree.root_node)
        for node, capture_name in captures:
            name = source[node.start_byte:node.end_byte].decode()
            if name == symbol:
                kind = 'function' if capture_name == 'fn.name' else 'class'
                results.append(SymbolLocation(
                    file=str(path),
                    line=node.start_point[0] + 1,
                    col=node.start_point[1],
                    kind=kind,
                ))
    return results


# Example usage
locs = find_definitions(Path('src'), 'UserRepository')
for loc in locs:
    print(f"{loc.kind} at {loc.file}:{loc.line}")
The simplest tool an agent can use for code understanding: a subprocess wrapper around ripgrep that returns structured results.

import subprocess, json
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class GrepMatch:
    file: str
    line: int
    text: str

def grep_codebase(pattern: str, root: str = '.', *, language: str = '') -> list[GrepMatch]:
    """
    Search for  (regex) across the codebase.
    Optionally filter by file extension via --type.
    Returns at most 100 matches to avoid overwhelming the context.
    """
    cmd = ['rg', '--json', '--max-count=100', pattern]
    if language:
        cmd += ['--type', language]   # e.g. 'python', 'typescript'
    cmd.append(root)

    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    matches: list[GrepMatch] = []
    for line in result.stdout.splitlines():
        obj = json.loads(line)
        if obj.get('type') == 'match':
            m = obj['data']
            matches.append(GrepMatch(
                file=m['path']['text'],
                line=m['line_number'],
                text=m['lines']['text'].rstrip(),
            ))
    return matches


# Find all call sites of a function
for m in grep_codebase(r'load_config\s*\(', language='python'):
    print(f"{m.file}:{m.line}  {m.text}")
Using the Language Server Protocol to resolve the type of a symbol at a specific file/line/column. This is the most reliable way to get type information in statically-typed languages.

import subprocess, json, threading, time
from pathlib import Path

class LspClient:
    """
    Minimal stdio LSP client for pyright / pylsp.
    Handles the JSON-RPC framing and basic request/response.
    """
    def __init__(self, command: list[str], root: Path):
        self._proc = subprocess.Popen(
            command,
            stdin=subprocess.PIPE,
            stdout=subprocess.PIPE,
            stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL,
        )
        self._pending: dict[int, dict] = {}
        self._lock = threading.Lock()
        self._seq = 0
        threading.Thread(target=self._reader, daemon=True).start()
        self._initialize(root)

    def _send(self, obj: dict) -> int:
        raw = json.dumps(obj)
        msg = f'Content-Length: {len(raw)}

{raw}'
        self._proc.stdin.write(msg.encode())
        self._proc.stdin.flush()
        return obj.get('id', -1)

    def _reader(self):
        while True:
            header = b''
            while not header.endswith(b'

'):
                header += self._proc.stdout.read(1)
            length = int(header.split(b'Content-Length: ')[1].split()[0])
            body = self._proc.stdout.read(length)
            obj = json.loads(body)
            if 'id' in obj:
                with self._lock:
                    self._pending[obj['id']] = obj

    def _wait(self, req_id: int, timeout: float = 5.0) -> dict:
        deadline = time.time() + timeout
        while time.time() < deadline:
            with self._lock:
                if req_id in self._pending:
                    return self._pending.pop(req_id)
            time.sleep(0.05)
        raise TimeoutError(f'LSP request {req_id} timed out')

    def _initialize(self, root: Path):
        self._seq += 1
        self._send({'jsonrpc': '2.0', 'id': self._seq, 'method': 'initialize',
                    'params': {'rootUri': root.as_uri(), 'capabilities': {}}})
        self._wait(self._seq)

    def hover(self, uri: str, line: int, character: int) -> str:
        """Return the hover text (type + docs) for the symbol at line/col."""
        self._seq += 1
        req_id = self._seq
        self._send({'jsonrpc': '2.0', 'id': req_id, 'method': 'textDocument/hover',
                    'params': {'textDocument': {'uri': uri},
                               'position': {'line': line - 1, 'character': character}}})
        response = self._wait(req_id)
        result = response.get('result')
        if result and result.get('contents'):
            c = result['contents']
            return c.get('value', '') if isinstance(c, dict) else str(c)
        return ''


# Usage
client = LspClient(['pyright-langserver', '--stdio'], Path('.'))
hover_text = client.hover(
    uri='file:///home/user/project/src/auth.py',
    line=42,
    character=15,
)
print(hover_text)  # e.g.: "(method) validate_token(token: str) -> TokenPayload"
Consider a coding agent asked to "migrate the
data class from a plain dict to a Pydantic v2
" in a Django REST API codebase with ~80,000 lines. Here is how its code understanding pipeline executes:
AST query: find the class definition named
File read of lines 34–60 shows a
LSP find-references returns 47 usage sites across 12 files: serializers, view functions, test fixtures, and migration scripts.
AST query across those 12 files finds all
call expressions. 38 use keyword arguments; 9 use
— this pattern is Pydantic-compatible, no change needed.
combined with assignment operators finds 6 sites that do
. Pydantic v2 models are immutable by default — the agent adds
to verify types, then runs the test suite. Both pass on the first attempt.

Without code understanding, the agent would have needed to guess which files were affected, missing the mutation sites and producing a change that failed tests. With it, the migration was correct on the first pass, touching exactly the 13 files that needed changing.
Relying on grep alone for semantic questions

Grepping for a class name to find its definition is unreliable in Python because the same name may appear in string literals, docstrings, comments, and type annotations. It also cannot distinguish the definition from a hundred import sites. Using tree-sitter to query specifically for a

# Wrong: matches comments, strings, imports, everything
subprocess.run(['rg', 'UserRecord', 'src'], capture_output=True)

# Right: AST query — only matches the class definition node
query = PY_LANGUAGE.query('(class_definition name: (identifier) @name)')
for node, _ in query.captures(tree.root_node):
    if source[node.start_byte:node.end_byte].decode() == 'UserRecord':
        print(f'Definition at line {node.start_point[0] + 1}')
Not invalidating stale AST caches after edits

If an agent parses a file at the start of a task and caches the AST, then edits the file mid-task, subsequent AST queries against the cached tree return stale results. The fix is to treat the parsed tree as a one-time artifact: parse fresh for each file you are about to query, or use tree-sitter's incremental parsing to apply edits as diffs.

Agents sometimes load entire files into the context window to "be safe". This approach fills context quickly in large codebases, leaving no room for the model's own reasoning or the output it needs to generate. Targeted retrieval — load only the lines containing the relevant function, plus its direct callers — consistently outperforms whole-file inclusion while using a fraction of the tokens.
Start with the cheapest tool that can answer the question (grep), escalate to AST or LSP only when the question requires structural or semantic precision. This minimises latency and tool-call budget.
Prefer narrowed reads over full files.
When you know a function spans lines 120–180 of a 2,000-line file, read lines 115–185 (a small surrounding margin). Never load the whole file unless the analysis genuinely requires the full content.
Build and reuse a symbol index at task start.

For tasks that will require multiple symbol lookups, run a one-time indexing pass over the relevant subtree at task startup rather than re-parsing on each lookup. The amortised cost is much lower for tasks touching many symbols.
Before writing a change, the agent should explicitly confirm: "I believe
. Let me verify with LSP hover before writing the caller." This step prevents confident-but-wrong code generation.
Use chunked embeddings for concept search.

Embed at the function-granularity level (one chunk per function or class), not at arbitrary token windows. Function-level embeddings retrieve semantically coherent units rather than arbitrary code fragments that may split a function in half.
Include test files in the understanding scope.

Tests are the most reliable source of intent and usage examples. An agent that understands how a function is tested has better context for modifying it safely than one that only reads the production code.

The performance characteristics of code understanding tools differ by more than an order of magnitude. Ripgrep scans at roughly 1–3 GB/s on SSD hardware — a 500,000-line Python codebase returns in under 100 ms. Tree-sitter parsing is also fast: the Python grammar parses at ~100 MB/s, so a 10,000-line file takes under 10 ms. LSP requests are slower because they involve inter-process communication and may trigger incremental type checking; a single hover or definition request typically takes 50–500 ms depending on the language server and the complexity of the type.

Vector embedding search scales horizontally. ANN indexes (FAISS, HNSW) return approximate nearest-neighbours over millions of chunks in 1–10 ms at query time. The bottleneck is index build time: embedding a large codebase with a hosted model requires API calls, which are rate-limited and may take minutes for a large repository. For self-hosted embedding models (e.g.,
on a GPU), full index builds take seconds for a typical project.

For monorepo-scale codebases (millions of files), partitioned indexes by subdirectory or service boundary are essential. The agent resolves which partition to query based on the path of the file currently under edit, avoiding full-index scans. Indexes must be updated on a file-change event basis to stay fresh; incremental updates are O(changed files), not O(entire repo).
Fastest text search; supports .gitignore; JSON output via --json
Incremental, error-tolerant; Python, Rust, Node bindings
Best Python LSP; also bundles a standalone analysis API
Full type checking via tsc; supports go-to-def, hover, find-refs
Exposes full macro expansion and trait resolution via LSP
ANN vector index for semantic code search over embeddings
Lightweight symbol index; good fallback for unsupported-LSP languages
Code understanding tools are generally read-only — they parse and index but do not execute the code. This makes them safe to run on untrusted repositories. However, some edge cases require attention.
Malicious repositories can exploit overly trusting build tools. Running
to resolve types for an LSP server is dangerous in untrusted code. Prefer sandboxed or pre-built language servers, and avoid installing dependencies from unknown repositories.

Embedding indexes store semantic representations of the code. If the index is hosted externally and shared across users (e.g. in a multi-tenant coding assistant), embedding leakage is a real concern. Proprietary algorithms can sometimes be approximately reconstructed from their embeddings. For proprietary codebases, keep the embedding index local or in a tenant-isolated store.

From a reliability standpoint, the main failure mode is index staleness. An agent that makes decisions based on a stale index may introduce changes that conflict with recent commits from other developers. A robust implementation tracks file modification timestamps (or git tree-sha hashes) and re-indexes any file that has changed since the last index build, running this check before each task rather than on a fixed schedule.

Language server crashes are another reliability concern. LSP servers occasionally crash or hang, especially on very large files or pathological inputs. The agent should run the language server as a subprocess with a watchdog: if a request times out or the server exits, restart it and retry rather than propagating the failure upward.
Code understanding operates in four layers — lexical, syntactic, semantic, and contextual — each answering progressively richer questions about the codebase.
Start with the cheapest tool that answers the question: grep for text patterns, tree-sitter for structural queries, LSP for semantic resolution, and embeddings for concept-level similarity.
Targeted retrieval — narrow reads, precise symbol lookups — outperforms full-file context loading in both token efficiency and model accuracy for large codebases.
LSP provides ground-truth type and reference data that is more reliable than anything the model can infer from its training knowledge, especially in actively evolving codebases.
Index staleness is the primary reliability failure mode: always validate that the index reflects the current on-disk state before acting on its results.
Embedding indexes should be per-tenant and locally stored for proprietary code; semantic representations can leak information even without exposing raw source.
Including tests in the understanding scope provides the clearest signal of intended behavior and is essential context for safe refactoring and API changes.
